schools.summary: Gill: this was upon one of the high places of Baal, after Balaam had viewed Israel encamped.
Henry: great preparation made for the cursing of Israel, aiming to engage the God of Israel to forsake them or stand neuter — the sottishness of superstition, to imagine God will be at men's beck.

themes: Build me here seven altars, and prepare me here seven oxen and seven rams
the costly attempt to curse Israel
imagining God could be hired to forsake his own

schools.summary: Gill: Balak ordered the seven altars built and the seven bullocks and rams prepared.
Keil & Delitzsch: Balak and Balaam offered on every altar a bullock and a ram.

themes: Balak and Balaam offered on every altar a bullock and a ram
sparing no cost in his enmity
a zeal in evil shaming coldness in good

schools.summary: Gill: Balak was to stand presenting himself and his offering to the Lord, that he might have respect to both, while Balaam went to seek what the LORD would show him.
Clarke: 'stand by thy burnt-offering' while Balaam went to inquire of the LORD.

themes: Stand by thy burnt offering, and I will go: peradventure the LORD will come to meet me
the diviner's helpless uncertainty
God meeting him to overrule his malice

schools.summary: Gill: God met him not in a kind or gracious manner, not out of respect to him or his offerings, but for the sake of his people Israel, to counterwork the designs of their enemies and confound their schemes.

themes: And God met Balaam
not in favour but for Israel's sake
overruling the wicked, the hired mouth turned to blessing

schools.summary: Gill: God put not grace into his heart nor the fear of God within him, but suggested what to say, impressing it with such power and weight that he was obliged to deliver it.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD put a word in Balaam's mouth and bid him return to Balak and speak it.

themes: the LORD put a word in Balaam's mouth... thus thou shalt speak
the man unchanged, only constrained
God using a wicked instrument to declare his truth

schools.summary: Gill: he returned immediately, as soon as he was told what to say; Balak and all the princes of Moab stood by the burnt sacrifice, awaiting the curse.

themes: he stood by his burnt sacrifice, he, and all the princes of Moab
the enemies poised to hear a curse
about to hear the LORD's blessing instead

schools.summary: Gill: the prophetic word is called a 'parable' because prophecies often use figurative, weighty, and sententious expressions.
Keil & Delitzsch: Balak fetched him from Aram, the mountains of the east, saying, Come, curse me Jacob.

themes: Balak... hath brought me from Aram... saying, Come, curse me Jacob
the weighty prophetic parable
the hired curser made to publish a blessing

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam had the will to curse, to gain Balak's money and honour, but saw it impossible — God does not curse his spiritual Israel, who are blessed in Christ, and it was vain to attempt what God would not have done (Rom. 8:31-33).

themes: How shall I curse, whom God hath not cursed?
no curse falling on the blessed
none can charge whom God has justified (Rom. 8:31-33)

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam, on the high hill, had a view of Israel encamped in the plains below.
Keil & Delitzsch: the people shall dwell alone, and shall not be reckoned among the nations.

themes: the people shall dwell alone, and shall not be reckoned among the nations
their distinctness their glory and safety
a peculiar people set apart for God (1 Pet. 2:9)

schools.summary: Gill: the dust of Jacob speaks of their numbers, as numerous as the dust of the earth, fulfilling the promise.
Clarke: Balaam had some presentiment that he should be cut off by a violent death, and wished rather to die the death of the righteous.

themes: Let me die the death of the righteous, and let my last end be like his!
longing for the saint's end without his life
the divided heart, dying at last by the sword (Num. 31:8)

schools.summary: Gill: Balak asks 'what hast thou done?' — nothing to answer his purpose. Calvin: the proud man reproaches the false prophet as if he had purchased a curse.
Ellicott: the Hebrew 'thou hast blessed, to bless' is an emphatic statement that Balaam uttered nothing but blessings.

themes: I took thee to curse mine enemies, and... thou hast blessed them altogether
the hired curse turned wholly to blessing
the schemes of the wicked recoiling

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam replied to Balak by way of defence.
Keil & Delitzsch: must he not take heed to speak that which the LORD had put in his mouth?

themes: Must I not take heed to speak that which the LORD hath put in my mouth?
a true principle from a false heart
God's mouthpiece cannot speak against his purpose

schools.summary: Gill: Balak, unable to help himself, was willing to try Balaam another time and elsewhere.
Henry: the second preparation for cursing, the place changed, Balak fancying the full prospect of the camp had hindered the curse.

themes: Come... unto another place... thou shalt see but the utmost part of them... curse me them from thence
the superstitious shifting of ground
as if God's will turned on the prophet's view

schools.summary: Gill: the field of Zophim was named from the watchmen, a high place for viewing armies.
Ellicott: the field of Zophim, 'of watchers,' near the spot from which Moses afterward surveyed the land.

themes: he brought him into the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah, and built seven altars
the same costly ritual repeated in vain
God not moved by the multiplying of altars

schools.summary: Gill: this was as he had directed before.
Keil & Delitzsch: Balaam went to meet the LORD yonder while Balak stood by his burnt offering.

themes: Stand here by thy burnt offering, while I meet the LORD yonder
the same ceremony repeated
the LORD's purpose toward Israel unchanged

schools.summary: Gill: it was as he did before — the LORD met him and put a word in his mouth.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD met Balaam, put a word in his mouth, and bid him go again to Balak.

themes: the LORD met Balaam, and put a word in his mouth
the second meeting as sovereign as the first
the same God, the same purpose

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: before it was 'all the princes,' but now only some, for seeing no hope of success, some had gone away.
Clarke: Balak now understood Balaam was wholly under the influence of Jehovah and would say nothing but what God gave him.

themes: What hath the LORD spoken?
the king's dawning surrender
Moab's malice breaking up before God's purpose

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam pronounced the word put into his mouth. Calvin: the elevated language and emphatic preface 'Rise up, Balak, and hear' are repetitions indicating something uncommon and awakening the hearer's attention.

themes: Rise up, Balak, and hear; hearken unto me, thou son of Zippor
the emphatic call to attention
preparing the king for an unwelcome truth

schools.summary: Gill: God is not to be conceived of corporeally, nor as a sinful man who goes astray and speaks lies; let God be true and every man a liar; what he has said he will surely do, what he has spoken he will make good (Heb. 6:18; Titus 1:2).

themes: God is not a man, that he should lie... hath he said, and shall he not do it?
the faithfulness and unchangeableness of God
impossible for God to lie (Heb. 6:18; Titus 1:2)

schools.summary: Gill: he had received commandment to bless the people of Israel, to declare them a happy people.
Keil & Delitzsch: Balaam meets Balak's expectation that he would take back the blessing with the declaration, 'I cannot reverse it.'

themes: I have received commandment to bless: and he hath blessed; and I cannot reverse it
the curse made impossible
what God blesses is blessed indeed (Rom. 11:29)

schools.summary: Gill: not that there was no sin in them, but God did not mark it in strict justice to punish, but forgave and did not impute it; the Targums restrain it to idolatry, none worshipping idols; the LORD their God was with them, the shout of a king among them (Rom. 4:8).

themes: He hath not beheld iniquity in Jacob... the LORD his God is with him
sin not imputed but covered
the gospel of justification (Rom. 4:8)

schools.summary: Gill: God who brought them out of Egypt with a mighty hand will conduct them through the wilderness and bring them safe to Canaan; it is vain to curse a people in such hands.
Keil & Delitzsch: God brought them out of Egypt; he has as it were the strength of the wild ox.

themes: God brought them out of Egypt; he hath as it were the strength of an unicorn
the Exodus the pledge of their keeping
vain to curse a people God delivered

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam here confesses all his enchantments and divinations signified nothing and could never bring a curse on Israel; it was vain for him to use them, and vain for Balak to expect anything; the wonder of God's work would be told, 'What hath God wrought!' (Isa. 54:17).

themes: there is no enchantment against Jacob, neither is any divination against Israel
the diviner's arts confessed bankrupt
no weapon formed against them shall prosper (Isa. 54:17)

schools.summary: Gill: read of the lioness, the strongest and most warlike beast, fierce and furious.
Keil & Delitzsch: the people shall rise up as a great lion to its prey.

themes: the people shall rise up as a great lion... he shall not lie down until he eat of the prey
Israel foretold to prevail, not be cursed
the Lion of Judah fulfilled in Christ (Rev. 5:5)

schools.summary: Gill: Balak meant it would be as well to do nothing as to do what he did; some render it, 'if thou wilt not curse, at least do not bless'; the king was galled by the repeated blessings.

themes: Neither curse them at all, nor bless them at all
the petulance of frustrated malice
the enemy reduced to a plea for silence

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam appeals for his honesty and faithfulness, that when they first met he plainly told Balak what to expect.
Keil & Delitzsch: told he not him that all the LORD speaks, that he must do?

themes: Told not I thee... All that the LORD speaketh, that I must do?
a true principle forced on a covetous heart
God holding the unwilling to obey his word

schools.summary: Gill: he would bring him to another place, if not better for the view, yet more religious, on which account he hoped for success.
Ellicott: Balak now names God as Elohim, seeming satisfied that Balaam was hindered by God from cursing.

themes: I will bring thee unto another place; peradventure it will please God that thou mayest curse
clutching at superstition to the last
unable to learn that what God blesses he will not curse

schools.summary: Gill: Peor was a high mountain in Moab where the idol Baal was worshipped, whence Baal-peor had his name, and probably a temple of Bethpeor stood, looking toward Jeshimon.
Clarke: probably the place where the famous Baal-peor had his chief temple.

themes: Balak brought Balaam unto the top of Peor, that looketh toward Jeshimon
the seat of Baal-worship
the dark place where Israel would later be ensnared (Num. 25:3)

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam was willing to try again, to gratify the king, and especially to get the wages of unrighteousness, which he dearly loved (2 Pet. 2:15).
Keil & Delitzsch: the sacrifices were the same as in the former cases.

themes: Build me here seven altars, and prepare me here seven bullocks and seven rams
persisting a third time for gain
held by the wages of unrighteousness he loved (2 Pet. 2:15)

schools.summary: Gill: though the sacrifices were expensive, Balak did not grudge them, sparing no cost to gain his point, though he could now have little hope of it.
Keil & Delitzsch: Balak did as Balaam had said, and offered a bullock and a ram on every altar.

themes: Balak did as Balaam had said, and offered a bullock and a ram on every altar
sparing no cost to the end
the blessing of God not to be turned into a curse
